title: Chapter 6: The Gallery
content: The shift hits like a held breath finally released.

One second I'm standing in my Murray Hill apartment — the one with the leaky faucet and the neighbor who plays saxophone at 2 AM — and the next I'm somewhere else entirely. Two days gone from this timeline, and my body knows it before my brain catches up. My feet ache in heels I didn't buy. My hair falls in waves I never learned to create.
I'm already dressed.

That's the part that keeps throwing me. Every time I shift, this version of myself has already done the work — chosen the outfit, applied the makeup, made whatever choices led to this exact moment. I look down at myself: black silk dress that skims my collarbones and lands just above the knee, architectural and severe except for the deep V in the back I can feel like a finger tracing my spine. Gold earrings that catch light I didn't know a room could hold. A beaded clutch that weighs almost nothing and somehow contains an entire alternate existence.
This Avery has impeccable taste I don't understand. My Avery — the real one, the Murray Hill one — buys dresses from sale racks and considers black pants a personality trait.

The gallery is called L'Ombre et La Lumière. Shadow and Light. Of course it is. It's in the Marais, which I know only because I checked the calendar on this Avery's phone in the cab, scrolling through appointments like reading someone else's diary. The building is all exposed stone and industrial lighting, the kind of space that costs more to look deliberately unfinished than most people spend on renovation.

The crowd moves like a single organism — beautiful people in beautiful clothes whispering beautiful lies. I catch fragments in French and English and something that might be Russian, the syllables floating on rivers of Sancerre and champagne. The air is thick with Chanel No. 5 and the mineral smell of oil paint and raw canvas, underlaid with something green and woody from the large arrangement of eucalyptus by the entrance. My tongue still holds the ghost of the wine I must have been drinking before I became conscious of being here — crisp, mineral, cold.
I take a glass from a passing tray. When in the Marais.
And then I see him.

Julian Deveraux. But not the Julian I met in the boardroom on the thirty-seventh floor. Not charcoal suits and glacier eyes and a voice that could freeze mercury. This Julian stands across the room in cream linen, the sleeves rolled to his forearms, one hand in his pocket while the other gestures as he speaks to an older woman in rapid French. His posture is relaxed but completely present — like a cat in a sunbeam, all softness until you remember the claws.

Same face. Same gray eyes. Same jaw that could cut glass. But everything else is different. Where Julian A was winter, this one is late summer. Where Julian A was a closed door, this one is a window left open to let the evening in. He radiates warmth like a controlled burn — contained fire, held purposefully behind some internal dam.
He hasn't seen me yet.

I stare. I can't help it. It's like seeing a familiar painting in a different frame. The composition is identical — the brushstrokes, the proportions, the way the light falls across the cheekbones — but the palette has shifted, and the whole piece reads differently. Warmer. Softer. More dangerous in an entirely new way.

The older woman touches his arm, says something that makes him smile — and God, that smile. Julian A's smile was a negotiation. This one is an event. It reaches his eyes and turns them the color of storm skies over the ocean.
Then he sees me.

The shift is physical. His whole body reorients. The conversation continues — his mouth is still moving, polite responses to whatever the woman is saying — but his attention has detached entirely and locked onto me like a compass finding north. He finishes speaking without breaking eye contact. Doesn't excuse himself. Doesn't perform the social choreography of departure. He just moves, threading through the crowd with the liquid competence of someone who's never had to fight for space in a room.
Heads turn as he passes. Of course they do. He's the kind of beautiful that makes people forget their sentences mid-word. But he's not looking at any of them.
He stops in front of me. Close enough that I can smell him — something warm and woody, sandalwood and bergamot, no cold notes at all — and I realize I was holding my breath.
"You came."

Two words. His voice is lower than Julian A's, the French warmth underneath the precision like embers beneath ash. And there's something in his eyes I didn't see in the boardroom: genuine pleasure. Raw and unperformed. Like my presence is a gift he wasn't sure he'd receive.
I'm in trouble.
"I said I would," I hear myself say, and it's the right response because his smile deepens, but I have no memory of making any such promise. This Avery's mouth knows things her mind never taught me.

He guides me deeper into the gallery with a hand at the small of my back — not possessive, just present, like he's making sure the room doesn't swallow me. We stop in front of a large canvas: black and gold, violence and light, the kind of painting that makes you feel something before you understand it.
"Tell me what you see," he says.
I look at the painting because it's easier than looking at him. "Chaos," I say. "But deliberate. Like the artist wanted to destroy something beautiful and couldn't quite commit."
His head tilts. The gray eyes sharpen. "That's almost exactly what Marchaud said about your thesis."
My what?

"The way you wrote about disruption as a creative force," he continues, watching me now the way the woman in the painting is watching something outside the frame. "About how systems don't break — they become. I thought it was the most interesting thing I'd read that year."

He's given me a rope, and I should grab it, but my hands are full of things I don't understand. My thesis. I wrote a thesis. This Avery wrote a thesis about disruption and systems, and I have absolutely no idea what it says.
"I've been thinking about expanding it," I say, deflecting. A safe bet — academics are always expanding things.
His head tilts further. A centimeter. Maybe two. "You don't sound like someone who wrote that paper." My pulse spikes. "You sound like someone who read it once and forgot."
Direct hit.
The words land exactly where he aimed them, and I see the moment of impact register in his eyes — not triumph, but curiosity. The cat has spotted movement in the grass.

I take a long drink of my Sancerre. The cold hits my tongue, bright and mineral, and I use the pause to rearrange my face into something that doesn't say I'm an impostor wearing my own skin. When I lower the glass, he's watching me with an expression I can't fully parse: amused. Interested. Slightly dangerous.
"You're different tonight," he says. Not an accusation. An observation.
"Different how?"
"Less certain." A pause. "I like it."

I don't know what to do with that. The Julian I knew — Julian A, the one in the charcoal suit — would have weaponized uncertainty. This one offers it back to me like a stone he's found and thinks I might want to hold.
Before I can respond, a new voice cuts between us like a blade through silk.
"Jules."

She arrives like weather — sudden, total, impossible to ignore. Pill-red lips. Hair the color of old honey swept up in a chignon that probably took an hour and looks like it took five minutes. Champagne in hand. Zeroing in on Julian B with the precision of a guided missile.
Celeste Montague.
She inserts herself into the space between us without acknowledging my existence. Not hostile — worse. Oblivious. As if I'm a piece of furniture that happens to be occupying volume.
"Your mother is asking for you. Something about the Dubois dinner." Her French accent wraps around the words like ribbon around a wound — decorative and constricting.
Julian doesn't move. His eyes stay on me. "I'm in the middle of a conversation, Céleste."
The use of her name is a door closing. He says it like someone who's said it many times before and has learned to say it without feeling.
Celeste finally turns to look at me.

The look is a complete anatomical dissection performed in half a second. She takes in the dress, the earrings, the clutch, the way I'm holding my glass. She catalogs my worth with the efficiency of an auctioneer and files it somewhere between ornamental and negligible. Then the smile arrives — honey and razor blades, sweet and cutting and designed to scar on removal.
"You must be the new consultant." Her gaze drifts over my shoulder, already moving on. "How... fresh."

The word lands like a slap delivered with a silk glove. Fresh. New. Unripe. Not ready. Not belonging. The temperature between us drops three degrees, and I feel it in the goosebumps rising on my bare arms and the cool silk suddenly cooler against my skin.
Julian's jaw tightens — the first hard thing I've seen from him. "Avery is a researcher, not a consultant. And she's considerably more interesting than anything you'd find fresh, Céleste."

But Celeste has already won. She's made me small in this room, in this dress, in this life I'm borrowing. She moves on like a shark that's tasted blood and found it uninteresting, and I'm left holding my glass and the sour aftertaste of having been dismissed by someone who didn't even bother to make it an insult.
Julian watches me process this. His expression is careful — not pitying, not apologetic. Just present.
"Come with me," he says.

He doesn't wait for an answer. He takes my glass and sets it on a passing tray, takes my hand, and leads me through the crowd and out a narrow door onto a balcony I didn't know existed. The rain hits before the view does — light, persistent, Paris rain, the kind that doesn't so much fall as decide to exist in the air around you.

The balcony is small. Barely room for two. Beyond the iron railing, the Seine stretches like molten glass, the city lights of Paris smearing across its surface in ribbons of gold and white and amber. The rain catches the light on its way down, and for a moment every drop is a tiny lantern suspended in dark water.

Julian shrugs off his jacket — cream linen, warm from his body — and drapes it over my shoulders without asking. Without ceremony. He doesn't wait for me to protest or accept. He just does it, like it's the only reasonable response to a woman standing in the rain in a silk dress.

The jacket is warm. I can feel his heat in the fabric, smell him more clearly now — sandalwood, bergamot, something underneath that's just him, skin and sweat and the faint mineral tang of rain. The cold silk of my dress pricks my arms; the warmth of his jacket wraps my shoulders. I'm held between two temperatures like a note between two chords.

We stand in silence. The rain makes lace on the railing. The Seine makes light where the city falls into it. My fingers curl into the jacket's sleeves, and I feel the weight of it — not heavy, but deliberate. A choice he made with his hands that he's not taking back.
"She's not my anything," he says. "For the record."

I look at him. Same face. Different man. The same architecture that made Julian A a weapon makes this Julian a shelter — not because he's softer, but because he's chosen to be. The effort is visible in the set of his shoulders, the way he leaves space between us, the way he told me about Celeste without asking me to care.
Same gravity, though. That's the part I can't escape. The pull. The way my body orients toward him like he's true north in a world where I've lost the map.
"For the record," I say, "I'm not anyone's anything either."
He smiles. Not the public smile from the gallery — this one is slower. Private. Like I've handed him a key he wasn't expecting, and he's turning it over in his mind, deciding which door it opens.
Rain collects on his eyelashes. He doesn't blink.

I should tell him something. That I'm not who he thinks I am. That the woman who wrote the thesis doesn't exist anymore, or maybe never existed at all, or exists in some Murray Hill apartment two days absent from this timeline. That I'm glitching through fates that were never mine, wearing lives like borrowed dresses, and the fit is never quite right.
But I don't.

Because right now, on this balcony, in this rain, in this jacket that smells like him — I'm real. More real than I've been since the app first glitched and the walls between worlds came down. More real than I was in the boardroom on the thirty-seventh floor, more real than I was in a cottage in Clonakilty with blood on my hands and a stranger's pulse under my thumb.

Here, with the Seine making lanterns of the city and the rain making music on the iron railing and his jacket warm on my shoulders — here, I'm not performing a version of myself. I'm just standing in it.
He turns back to the view. I turn with him. The rain doesn't stop. Neither do we.

Later — minutes or hours, I can't tell — I excuse myself to the bathroom. I lock the door. I lean against the sink and stare at a face in the mirror that's mine but better, half-visible through the fog of someone else's skincare routine, and I reach into the borrowed clutch on the counter.
The Fork app opens.
Three paths glow on the screen. Three timelines. Three men.
Wall Street demon. Charcoal suit and glacier eyes, the one who took me apart in a boardroom and didn't bother to put me back together.
Paris sphinx. Warm linen and contained fire, the one who handed me his jacket and told me the truth about a woman I didn't need to ask about.
Irish bleeding stranger. The one whose pulse I held in my hands. The one who smiled at me — broken, small, real — and didn't know my name.
Same face. Same pull. Different armor.

All three paths are lit. All three are pulling me — three gravity wells in a universe that's come undone at the seams, each one dragging me toward a different collision. I feel the tension between them like a wire pulled taut in my chest, vibrating with the frequency of choices I haven't made yet.
I close the app without choosing.
For now.
